text:
MRI NECK WITH AND WITHOUT CONTRAST
PROTOCOL: Dedicated head and neck MRI with gadolinium contrast
INDICATION: 54-year-old female with history of HPV-positive squamous cell carcinoma, left base of tongue (T2N1M0), treated with definitive concurrent cisplatin and IMRT completing approximately 24 months prior. Presenting for 2-year post-treatment surveillance imaging. Evaluate for locoregional recurrence.
COMPARISON: MRI neck with contrast from approximately 12 months prior (12-month surveillance); original staging MRI from approximately 24 months prior.

TECHNIQUE: Multiplanar multisequence MRI of the neck performed without and with intravenous gadolinium-based contrast agent (gadobutrol, 0.1 mmol/kg). Sequences include axial T1, axial and coronal T2 fat-saturated, axial diffusion-weighted imaging with ADC mapping, and post-contrast T1 fat-saturated sequences in axial, coronal, and sagittal planes. Field of view encompassed skull base through thoracic inlet.

FINDINGS:

Primary Site - Left Base of Tongue:
The left base of tongue demonstrates stable post-treatment mucosal thickening and mild T2 signal heterogeneity, unchanged in character and extent when compared with the prior surveillance MRI from approximately 12 months ago. There is no discrete enhancing mass, no new nodular enhancement, and no erosion or infiltrative signal change extending into the intrinsic tongue musculature or pre-epiglottic space. The region of prior treated tumor shows expected post-radiation fibrotic changes: mild T2 hypointensity within the soft tissue at the left base of tongue and left vallecula, which is stable. No restricted diffusion to suggest dense cellularity or viable tumor. The right base of tongue and right vallecula appear within normal limits.

Oropharynx / Hypopharynx:
The bilateral tonsillar fossae, soft palate, and posterior pharyngeal wall are unremarkable. No mucosal thickening or abnormal enhancement along the posterior oropharyngeal wall. Pyriform sinuses symmetric and patent. No hypopharyngeal mass or abnormal enhancement.

Larynx:
The supraglottis, glottis, and subglottis demonstrate normal morphology. The epiglottis, aryepiglottic folds, arytenoids, true and false vocal cords appear intact without infiltration or abnormal enhancement. Pre-epiglottic and paraglottic fat spaces preserved.

Cervical Lymph Nodes:
No pathologically enlarged cervical lymph nodes identified bilaterally. The previously described left level IIA lymph node which had measured 25 mm at staging, decreased to 8 mm at 12-week post-treatment imaging, and subsequently to 5 mm at 12-month surveillance, is no longer discretely identifiable as a distinct nodal structure -- the region shows only expected post-treatment fibrofatty changes without any residual discrete soft tissue nodularity. No abnormal enhancement or restricted diffusion in the expected left level IIA nodal station. Bilateral levels IB, IIB, III, IV, and VA/VB lymph node stations are within normal limits with no nodes meeting size criteria for pathological enlargement and no abnormal morphology.

Soft Tissues of the Neck:
Diffuse radiation-related soft tissue changes are present in the bilateral neck, manifesting as mild increased T2 signal and mild diffuse subcutaneous thickening, stable and expected for this post-treatment interval. The strap muscles and sternocleidomastoid muscles show mild bilateral volume loss and increased T2 signal consistent with radiation-induced atrophy and fibrosis, unchanged from the prior study. No focal soft tissue mass. No abnormal rim-enhancing fluid collection to suggest abscess or necrosis. No fat stranding or infiltrative process.

Salivary Glands:
The parotid glands demonstrate moderate bilateral volume loss and fatty replacement consistent with radiation-induced changes; this is stable compared to prior MRI. Submandibular glands are mildly atrophic bilaterally. No ductal dilatation. No focal masses within the major salivary glands. These findings are expected given prior bilateral neck irradiation.

Mandible and Skull Base:
The mandible is intact without marrow signal abnormality, cortical disruption, or features to suggest osteoradionecrosis. The temporomandibular joints are symmetric. Skull base osseous structures appear intact without erosion or abnormal marrow signal.

Thyroid Gland:
The thyroid gland appears mildly heterogeneous and reduced in volume compared to the pre-treatment baseline, consistent with radiation-induced thyroid injury. No discrete thyroid nodules meeting criteria for further evaluation. No abnormal thyroid enhancement.

Vascular Structures:
The carotid arteries are patent bilaterally. There is mild, stable intimal thickening of the bilateral common and internal carotid arteries, most pronounced at the carotid bifurcations, unchanged from prior imaging. No new significant interval change in the degree of wall thickening. No flow-limiting stenosis identified. These findings are consistent with known radiation-induced accelerated atherosclerosis and were previously noted.

IMPRESSION:

1. No evidence of locoregional recurrence at the left base of tongue primary site or in any cervical nodal station. The post-treatment changes at the left base of tongue are stable and consistent with expected radiation-induced fibrosis and mucosal remodeling.

2. Complete resolution of the previously described left level IIA soft tissue nodularity; no residual nodal abnormality is identified at this location.

3. No new cervical lymphadenopathy. No suspicious adenopathy identified bilaterally.

4. Stable radiation-related soft tissue and salivary gland changes in the bilateral neck, without evidence of radiation-induced necrosis or osteonecrosis of the mandible.

5. Mild, stable carotid intimal thickening bilaterally, unchanged from prior, in keeping with known post-irradiation vascular changes.

Overall, the examination is reassuring at this 2-year post-treatment interval, with no imaging findings to raise concern for residual or recurrent disease.
